name: Zenefits
summary: HR and payroll software startup hit a $4.5B valuation before revelations that employees used a script to fake state-mandated insurance licensing courses; the scandal forced the CEO out.
lesson: Growth-at-all-costs culture can create compliance shortcuts that unravel a company's valuation overnight.

name: InVision
summary: Design collaboration platform reached a $1.9B valuation but lost most of its user base to Figma's real-time collaborative editor, leading to major layoffs.
lesson: A market leader can be dethroned quickly if a competitor removes a fundamental workflow friction.

name: Docker Inc
summary: Container technology pioneer whose developer tool became an industry standard, but whose enterprise sales business failed to generate proportional revenue; sold its enterprise unit to Mirantis.
lesson: Ubiquitous open-source adoption doesn't guarantee a company can capture commensurate enterprise revenue.

name: Convoy
summary: Digital freight brokerage/logistics software platform backed by top-tier investors abruptly shut down after a slump in freight volumes and failed acquisition talks.
lesson: Even well-capitalized marketplace startups are exposed to macro cyclicality in the industries they digitize.

name: Bolt Financial
summary: One-click checkout SaaS reached an $11B valuation before layoffs and public controversy over founder claims about funding and metrics led to a collapse in its valuation.
lesson: Founder credibility problems can destroy enterprise sales momentum even when the core product works.

name: Hopin
summary: Virtual-events SaaS platform rocketed to a $7.75B valuation during the pandemic, then saw demand collapse as in-person events returned; sold its core product to RingCentral for a fraction of capital raised.
lesson: Pandemic-driven demand spikes can reverse just as fast, punishing companies that scaled to match the peak.

name: ScaleFactor
summary: 'AI-powered' small-business bookkeeping SaaS shut down after a Forbes investigation revealed its automation was largely manual human bookkeeping that frequently produced errors.
lesson: Marketing a product as AI-automated when it isn't reliably working can destroy trust once customers are exposed to the failures.

name: Fast
summary: One-click checkout startup raised $102M just months before abruptly shutting down, becoming a symbol of 2022's startup pullback after burning cash on rapid, unprofitable expansion.
lesson: Fast growth funded by cheap capital can collapse just as quickly once funding conditions tighten.

name: IronNet
summary: Cybersecurity firm founded by a former NSA director went public via SPAC, then filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after failing to convert government pedigree into sustained commercial revenue.
lesson: Prestigious founders and government contacts don't guarantee a repeatable commercial sales motion.

name: Quixey
summary: Mobile app search engine raised over $200M including from SoftBank, but shut down amid allegations that executives had misrepresented revenue figures to investors.
lesson: Inflating metrics to secure large late-stage rounds eventually catches up with a company.

name: Evernote
summary: Pioneering notes app once reached a $1B+ valuation but suffered years of stagnation and declining relevance against Notion, culminating in its 2023 sale to Bending Spoons.
lesson: Early category leadership can erode without continued product innovation and focus.

name: Namely
summary: HR and payroll software for mid-sized companies raised over $260M before being sold to Vensure Employer Services after struggling to grow into its valuation.
lesson: Raising at aggressive valuations can force an eventual down-market exit if growth doesn't keep pace.

name: Percolate
summary: Content marketing and brand management SaaS raised over $135M but was sold to Seismic in what was widely reported as a distressed, down-round acquisition.
lesson: Large enterprise contracts don't guarantee unit economics that support the cash burned to win them.

name: Slack
summary: Team messaging platform pivoted from a failed game studio into the fastest-growing SaaS company at the time, ultimately acquired by Salesforce in 2021 for $27.7B.
lesson: A well-executed pivot informed by internal tooling can create a category-defining product.

name: Zoom
summary: Video conferencing SaaS IPO'd in 2019 and saw explosive growth during the COVID-19 pandemic on the strength of its reliability and ease of use.
lesson: Superior reliability in a mundane, unglamorous feature can beat entrenched incumbents.

name: Atlassian
summary: Australian enterprise software company grew profitably for over a decade without a traditional sales team before its 2015 IPO.
lesson: A self-serve, product-led growth motion can out-scale traditional enterprise sales.

name: HubSpot
summary: Inbound marketing and CRM SaaS platform pioneered the 'inbound marketing' category, went public in 2014, and has grown into a multi-billion dollar public company.
lesson: Creating and owning a marketing category can be as valuable as the product itself.

name: Salesforce
summary: Pioneer of the SaaS/cloud CRM model grew from a scrappy challenger into the dominant enterprise CRM platform and one of the largest software companies in the world.
lesson: Betting early and loudly on a new delivery model can define an entire industry.

name: Shopify
summary: E-commerce platform-as-a-service started as a snowboard shop's software and grew into critical infrastructure for millions of online merchants, going public in 2015.
lesson: Building the tool you needed yourself can uncover a much larger addressable market.

name: Twilio
summary: Developer-first API platform for communications became foundational infrastructure for thousands of applications, going public in 2016.
lesson: Making a complex capability simple for developers can create durable platform lock-in.

name: Snowflake
summary: Cloud data warehousing platform that separated storage and compute for cloud-native analytics went public in 2020 in the largest software IPO in history at the time.
lesson: Re-architecting a legacy category for the cloud can unlock enormous enterprise demand.

name: CrowdStrike
summary: Cloud-native endpoint security platform replaced legacy signature-based antivirus with a lightweight agent and cloud analytics, going public in 2019.
lesson: A cloud-native architecture can leapfrog legacy incumbents stuck with on-premise designs.

name: Figma
summary: Browser-based, real-time collaborative design tool displaced Adobe and Sketch in product design workflows; a planned $20B Adobe acquisition was abandoned due to regulatory opposition, and Figma continued operating successfully as an independent company.
lesson: Real-time multiplayer collaboration can be a defensible wedge that topples entrenched desktop-software incumbents.

name: Notion
summary: All-in-one workspace/notes/wiki/database tool built a passionate community-led user base and reached a roughly $10B valuation while remaining private.
lesson: A flexible, composable tool can win a devoted community that becomes its own growth engine.

name: Rippling
summary: HR, IT, and payroll SaaS platform founded by Zenefits co-founder Parker Conrad unifies employee data across systems, growing into one of the most highly valued private SaaS companies.
lesson: A founder can rebuild credibility and a bigger company by directly addressing the architectural gap that caused a prior failure.

name: GitHub
summary: Code hosting and collaboration platform became the default home for open-source and enterprise software development, acquired by Microsoft in 2018 for $7.5B.
lesson: Owning the default workflow of a large, high-value user base creates enormous strategic value.

name: Mailchimp
summary: Email marketing SaaS grew from a side project into a bootstrapped, profitable company serving millions of small businesses without ever raising venture capital, before being acquired by Intuit for $12B.
lesson: A profitable, self-funded company can still command a massive acquisition price without ever raising VC money.

name: Datadog
summary: Cloud monitoring and observability SaaS rode the shift to cloud-native infrastructure to a successful 2019 IPO and continued strong growth as a public company.
lesson: Aligning a product with a broad infrastructure shift can drive years of tailwind growth.
